title: Welcome to My Knowledge Base
text:
This is a personal knowledge management wiki built with TiddlyWiki. It contains notes on various topics including science, technology, philosophy, and project management.

!! Getting Started

Use the sidebar to navigate between tiddlers. Click the ''+ New Tiddler'' button to create new content.

title: The Standard Model of Particle Physics
text:
The Standard Model of particle physics is the theory describing three of the four known fundamental forces (electromagnetic, weak, and strong interactions) in the universe and classifying all known elementary particles.

!! Fundamental Particles

The model includes:

* ''Quarks'': up, down, charm, strange, top, bottom
* ''Leptons'': electron, muon, tau (and their neutrinos)
* ''Gauge Bosons'': photon, W/Z bosons, gluons
* ''Higgs Boson'': discovered at CERN in 2012

!! Forces

|!Force |!Carrier |!Relative Strength |
|Electromagnetic |Photon |1/137 |
|Weak Nuclear |W/Z Bosons |10^^-6^^ |
|Strong Nuclear |Gluons |1 |
|Gravity |Graviton (theoretical) |10^^-39^^ |

The Standard Model has been remarkably successful in predicting experimental results, but it does not incorporate gravity or explain dark matter and dark energy.

See also: [[Quantum Entanglement Explained]] | [[General Relativity and Spacetime]]

title: Quantum Entanglement Explained
text:
Quantum entanglement is a phenomenon in quantum physics where two or more particles become interconnected in such a way that the quantum state of each particle cannot be described independently of the state of the others.

!! Key Concepts

* ''Superposition'': Particles exist in multiple states simultaneously until measured
* ''Measurement Problem'': Observing a particle collapses its wave function
* ''Non-locality'': Entangled particles affect each other instantaneously regardless of distance
* ''Bell's Theorem'': Proved that no local hidden variable theory can reproduce all predictions of quantum mechanics

!! EPR Paradox

Albert Einstein, Boris Podolsky, and Nathan Rosen proposed the EPR paradox in 1935, arguing that quantum mechanics was incomplete. Einstein famously called entanglement "spooky action at a distance."

John Bell's 1964 theorem and subsequent experiments by Alain Aspect (1982) confirmed that quantum entanglement is real and cannot be explained by local hidden variables.

!! Applications

# Quantum Computing
# Quantum Cryptography (QKD)
# Quantum Teleportation
# Quantum Sensing

Related: [[The Standard Model of Particle Physics]]

title: General Relativity and Spacetime
text:
General relativity (GR) is the geometric theory of gravitation published by Albert Einstein in 1915. It is the current description of gravitation in modern physics.

!! Core Principles

* ''Equivalence Principle'': Gravitational and inertial mass are equivalent
* ''Curved Spacetime'': Massive objects curve the fabric of spacetime
* ''Geodesics'': Objects in free fall follow curved paths (geodesics) through spacetime

!! Predictions Confirmed

# Gravitational lensing of light around massive objects
# Gravitational time dilation (GPS satellites must account for this)
# Gravitational waves (detected by LIGO in 2015)
# Black holes (imaged by the Event Horizon Telescope in 2019)
# Perihelion precession of Mercury's orbit

!! Einstein Field Equations

<<<
G_uv + Lambda * g_uv = (8 * pi * G / c^4) * T_uv
<<<

Where G_uv is the Einstein tensor describing spacetime curvature, Lambda is the cosmological constant, and T_uv is the stress-energy tensor.

Related: [[Quantum Entanglement Explained]] | [[The Standard Model of Particle Physics]]

title: CRISPR Gene Editing
text:
CRISPR-Cas9 is a revolutionary gene-editing technology adapted from a naturally occurring genome editing system in bacteria. Bacteria capture snippets of DNA from invading viruses and use them to create DNA segments known as CRISPR arrays.

!! How It Works

# A guide RNA (gRNA) is designed to match the target DNA sequence
# The Cas9 protein acts as molecular scissors
# The gRNA directs Cas9 to the precise location in the genome
# Cas9 cuts both strands of the DNA
# The cell's repair mechanisms fix the break, allowing insertion or deletion of genetic material

!! Applications

* ''Medicine'': Treating genetic disorders like sickle cell disease, beta thalassemia
* ''Agriculture'': Creating disease-resistant crops, improving nutritional content
* ''Research'': Understanding gene function, creating animal models of disease
* ''Diagnostics'': SHERLOCK and DETECTR systems for pathogen detection

!! Ethical Considerations

* Germline editing (heritable changes) raises profound ethical questions
* The 2018 case of He Jiankui editing human embryos sparked global controversy
* WHO Expert Advisory Committee established guidelines in 2021

Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier received the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for developing CRISPR-Cas9.

Related: [[Mitochondrial DNA and Human Migration]]

title: Mitochondrial DNA and Human Migration
text:
Mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) is a powerful tool for tracing human migration patterns because it is inherited exclusively from the mother and mutates at a relatively constant rate.

!! Key Findings

* ''Mitochondrial Eve'': All living humans share a common maternal ancestor who lived approximately 150,000-200,000 years ago in Africa
* ''Out of Africa'': mtDNA evidence supports the theory that modern humans migrated out of Africa approximately 70,000 years ago
* ''Haplogroups'': Distinct mtDNA lineages that correspond to geographic regions and migration routes

!! Major Haplogroups

|!Haplogroup |!Region |!Approximate Age |
|L0 |Southern Africa |150,000+ years |
|L1-L6 |Sub-Saharan Africa |Various |
|M |South/East Asia |60,000 years |
|N |West Eurasia |65,000 years |
|A, B, C, D |Americas |15,000-20,000 years |

!! Migration Routes

# Africa to Middle East (~70,000 years ago)
# Coastal route to South/Southeast Asia (~65,000 years ago)
# Into Europe (~45,000 years ago)
# Into the Americas via Beringia (~15,000-20,000 years ago)
# Into Oceania/Pacific Islands (~3,000-5,000 years ago)

Related: [[CRISPR Gene Editing]]

title: Agile Methodology Overview
text:
Agile software development refers to a group of software development methodologies based on iterative development, where requirements and solutions evolve through collaboration between self-organizing cross-functional teams.

!! Agile Manifesto Values (2001)

# ''Individuals and interactions'' over processes and tools
# ''Working software'' over comprehensive documentation
# ''Customer collaboration'' over contract negotiation
# ''Responding to change'' over following a plan

!! Common Frameworks

!!! Scrum
* Sprint-based development (2-4 week cycles)
* Roles: Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team
* Ceremonies: Sprint Planning, Daily Standup, Sprint Review, Retrospective

!!! Kanban
* Visual workflow management
* Work-in-progress (WIP) limits
* Continuous flow instead of fixed iterations

!!! Extreme Programming (XP)
* Pair programming
* Test-driven development
* Continuous integration
* Short release cycles

Related: [[Version Control Best Practices]] | [[Project Management]]

title: Version Control Best Practices
text:
Version control systems (VCS) are tools that help manage changes to source code over time. Git is the most widely used distributed version control system.

!! Git Branching Strategies

!!! Git Flow
* ''main'': Production-ready code
* ''develop'': Integration branch for features
* ''feature/*'': Individual feature branches
* ''release/*'': Release preparation
* ''hotfix/*'': Emergency production fixes

!!! Trunk-Based Development
* Short-lived feature branches (< 2 days)
* Frequent merges to main branch
* Feature flags for incomplete features
* Emphasis on continuous integration

!! Commit Message Conventions

```
type(scope): brief description

Longer explanation of the change.

Fixes #123
```

Types: feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, test, chore

!! Best Practices

# Commit early and often with atomic changes
# Write meaningful commit messages
# Never commit secrets or credentials
# Use .gitignore for build artifacts and dependencies
# Review code before merging (pull requests)
# Tag releases with semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH)

Related: [[Agile Methodology Overview]]

title: Knowledge and Justified True Belief
text:
The classical analysis of knowledge defines it as ''justified true belief'' (JTB). This definition has been the standard account since Plato's //Theaetetus//.

!! The Three Conditions

For a subject S to know that a proposition P:

# ''Truth'': P must be true
# ''Belief'': S must believe that P
# ''Justification'': S must have adequate justification for believing P

!! The Gettier Problem (1963)

Edmund Gettier showed that JTB is not sufficient for knowledge by constructing counterexamples:

<<<
Smith has strong evidence that Jones will get a job. Smith also sees Jones counting coins and believes Jones has 10 coins. In fact, Smith gets the job, and Smith himself happens to have 10 coins. Smith's belief "the person who will get the job has 10 coins" is justified, true, but not knowledge.
<<<

!! Responses to Gettier

* ''No False Lemmas'': Add condition that justification doesn't rely on false beliefs
* ''Defeasibility Theory'': No defeating evidence that undermines justification
* ''Reliabilism'': Belief must be produced by a reliable cognitive process
* ''Virtue Epistemology'': Knowledge requires intellectual virtue

!! Contemporary Debate

Some epistemologists have moved away from analyzing knowledge entirely, focusing instead on understanding, wisdom, or epistemic responsibility.

Related: [[Philosophy Readings]]

title: Database Normalization
text:
Database normalization is a systematic approach to organizing data in a relational database to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity.

!! Normal Forms

!!! First Normal Form (1NF)
* Each column contains only atomic (indivisible) values
* Each column contains values of a single type
* Each row is unique (has a primary key)

!!! Second Normal Form (2NF)
* Already in 1NF
* All non-key attributes are fully dependent on the entire primary key
* Eliminates partial dependencies in composite keys

!!! Third Normal Form (3NF)
* Already in 2NF
* No transitive dependencies: non-key attributes depend only on the primary key
* Example: Remove ''zip_code → city'' dependency from a customer table

!!! Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF)
* A stricter version of 3NF
* Every determinant is a candidate key

!! Denormalization

Sometimes intentional denormalization is used for:
* ''Performance'': Reducing JOIN operations in read-heavy workloads
* ''Reporting'': Star/snowflake schemas in data warehouses
* ''Caching'': Materializing frequently accessed computed values

!! Best Practices

# Start with 3NF and denormalize only when performance requires it
# Document all intentional denormalization decisions
# Use foreign key constraints to maintain referential integrity
# Consider indexing strategies alongside normalization level
